topic: An employee discovers the company is being sold
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights in the open-plan office seemed to hum at a frequency just slightly off-key today. Elias stared at the whiteboard, tracing the faded outline of the Q3 projections, but the numbers swam before his eyes. Every familiar corner of the cubicle farm felt subtly shifted, like a poorly rendered video game environment. He kept touching his tie knot, a nervous, repetitive motion. Sarah from Marketing walked past, her cheerful greeting sounding brittle, and Elias managed a tight, practiced smile that didn't reach his eyes. He felt a strange lightness in his chest, a vacuum where routine used to be. When he finally logged into the internal portal, the company structure looked… rearranged. Departments were listed under unfamiliar, abstract headings. A cold knot tightened in his stomach as he scrolled, realizing the familiar architecture of his workplace had been fundamentally redrawn.

[story 2]

“Just a weird afternoon,” Mark muttered into his lukewarm coffee, swirling the brown liquid with unnecessary vigor. He kept glancing at the framed company awards on the wall, suddenly finding their gleaming surfaces mocking him. The air in the breakroom felt thick, heavy, pressing down on his shoulders. He kept re-reading the email chain he’d accidentally saved—a terse, coded message about 'strategic realignment.' His hands began to tremble slightly as he tried to focus on his keyboard. It wasn't a sudden shock, more like the slow, creeping realization that the foundation beneath his feet was dissolving. He stood up abruptly, needing air, and found himself walking to the window, watching the cityscape blur, unable to anchor himself to the solid reality of the view outside.

[story 3]

“So, this is it, then?” Lena asked, her voice unnervingly flat as she leaned against the filing cabinet. She didn't look at him; her gaze was fixed on the polished floor tiles. He felt a strange detachment, as if he were observing the scene from a distance, watching a play where he had forgotten his lines. The usual buzz of activity in the office—the phones ringing, the hurried footsteps—seemed distant, muffled, as if underwater. He picked up a pen, turning it over and over until the plastic felt slick against his palm. The details of his immediate surroundings—the specific placement of the water cooler, the way the afternoon sun hit the dusty partition—were becoming indistinct, blurring into a wash of muted color. A profound sense of unmooring settled deep within him.
